1:
"And the ark of the LORD was in the country of the Philistines seven months." The very brief verse notes the ark remaining seven months among the Philistines. The grammatical voices note the time. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the ark of the LORD was in the country of the Philistines seven months" — the ark stayed seven months, the plague continuing; the and the ark of the LORD was in the country of the Philistines (in the country); the seven months (the time); the figure (the ark a plague to its captors for the whole season, God afflicting them till they let it go; the truth that the ark was seven months among the Philistines, the plague enduring, Psalm 105:14; the figure of God's hand pressing the foe until he releases the holy thing; the seven months; the ark's stay, vv.5:11). The verse holds together the time. The verse displays the seven months during which the ark, though captive, was a continual scourge to the Philistines, until they were forced to seek its return. The verse displays the truth that God can make even his captured cause a torment to his enemies until they release it in fear.

9:
"And see, if it goeth up by the way of his own coast to Bethshemesh, then he hath done us this great evil: but if not, then we shall know that it is not his hand that smote us; it was a chance that happened to us." The witnesses note the Philistines' test, whether the kine would bear the ark to Bethshemesh of themselves or it was chance — the crux of the question whether the hand of God or mere chance rules in the events that befall men. On "if it goeth up by the way of his own coast... then he hath done us this great evil: but if not... it was a chance," interpreters expound the test. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the proof between God's hand and chance — "and see, if it goeth up by the way of his own coast to Bethshemesh, then he hath done us this great evil: but if not, then we shall know that it is not his hand that smote us; it was a chance that happened to us": the Philistines devise a decisive test to learn whether God or chance had afflicted them; if therefore against all nature they went straight up the road to Bethshemesh in Israel, drawing the ark, it could be nothing but the hand of God overruling them, and the Philistines would know certainly that their plagues were his stroke and no chance; the witnesses note that the very heathen here reason rightly, that what is against nature and according to God's purpose is the proof of his hand, and that to ascribe such things to chance is the refuge of unbelief (the test that nature could not pass but God could overrule; the hand of God distinguished from chance; the heathen owning that God's overruling is the proof); the lesson — that nothing comes by chance, but the hand of God overrules all events, and the believer discerns his hand where the unbeliever sees only accident, "the lot is cast into the lap; but the whole disposing thereof is of the LORD," Proverbs 16:33; the truth that not a sparrow falls without the Father, and all things are ordered by his providence, "are not two sparrows sold for a farthing? and one of them shall not fall on the ground without your Father," Matthew 10:29; the figure — the overruling providence of God that governs all events, even the course of beasts, fulfilled in him who works all things after the counsel of his own will, "who worketh all things after the counsel of his own will," Ephesians 1:11. The figure of the question whether the hand of God or mere chance rules in the events that befall men.

On "And see, if it goeth up by the way of his own coast to Bethshemesh, then he hath done us this great evil," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the test of the hand of God. Keil and Ellicott note the Philistines testing whether the kine would go straight to Bethshemesh against nature, proving the hand of God (the test of God's hand, Proverbs 16:33) — the test (if it goeth to Bethshemesh).

On "but if not, then we shall know that it is not his hand that smote us; it was a chance that happened to us," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the alternative of chance. Calvin and Gill note the Philistines reasoning that if the kine turned back it was chance, but the untrained nursing cows going straight up against all nature could be nothing but God's overruling, not a sparrow falling without the Father (the hand of God against chance, Matthew 10:29; Ephesians 1:11) — the figure (the overruling providence of God that governs all events); the providence (the test, the kine going straight to Bethshemesh to follow, vv.10-12). The verse displays the test between God's hand and chance.

The witnesses agree the substance: the Philistines devise a decisive test to learn whether God or chance had afflicted them (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of God's hand or chance: then he hath done us this great evil: but if not... it was a chance — the shrewd test, for the kine were untrained, nursing mothers parted from their calves, and by nature would have turned back, so that if against all nature they went straight to Bethshemesh drawing the ark it could be nothing but the hand of God overruling, the very heathen reasoning rightly that what is against nature and according to God's purpose is the proof of his hand, that nothing comes by chance but the hand of God overrules all (Proverbs 16:33), not a sparrow falling without the Father (Matthew 10:29), God working all things after the counsel of his will (Ephesians 1:11). The verse displays the hand-or-chance crux. The test between God's hand and chance displays the crux: if it goeth up by the way of his own coast to Bethshemesh, then he hath done us this great evil: but if not... it was a chance (Proverbs 16:33; Matthew 10:29; Ephesians 1:11) — the question whether the hand of God or mere chance rules in the events that befall men, the overruling providence proved where nature could not act, faith seeing God's hand where unbelief sees only accident. The verse displays the kine going straight to Bethshemesh, lowing as they went (vv.10-12).

19:
"And he smote the men of Bethshemesh, because they had looked into the ark of the LORD, even he smote of the people fifty thousand and threescore and ten men: and the people lamented, because the LORD had smitten many of the people with a great slaughter." The witnesses note that God smote the men of Bethshemesh for looking into the ark — the crux of the holiness of God that will not be profaned even by his own people, and the reverence his presence demands. On "he smote the men of Bethshemesh, because they had looked into the ark of the LORD," interpreters expound the judgment. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the holiness of God that demands reverence — "and he smote the men of Bethshemesh, because they had looked into the ark of the LORD... and the people lamented, because the LORD had smitten many of the people with a great slaughter": God struck the men of Bethshemesh dead for irreverently prying into the ark; the witnesses note that the judgment fell not because they rejoiced in the ark but because they treated the holy with irreverent boldness (the holiness that demands reverence from God's own people; the curiosity that profanes the holy; the mercy turned to judgment by irreverence); the lesson — that God is greatly to be feared and reverenced in the assembly of his saints, and his holy things not to be presumed upon, "God is greatly to be feared in the assembly of the saints, and to be had in reverence of all them that are about him," Psalm 89:7; the truth that to draw near to God irreverently is to provoke his judgment, as Nadab and Abihu, "I will be sanctified in them that come nigh me," Leviticus 10:3; the figure — the holiness of God that must be approached with reverence and godly fear, fulfilled in the worship that serves God acceptably, "let us have grace, whereby we may serve God acceptably with reverence and godly fear: for our God is a consuming fire," Hebrews 12:28-29. The figure of the holiness of God that will not be profaned even by his own people, and the reverence his presence demands.

On "And he smote the men of Bethshemesh, because they had looked into the ark of the LORD," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the judgment for irreverence. Calvin and Keil note God smiting the men of Bethshemesh for prying with unholy curiosity into the ark which even the Levites might not look into, the holiness of God vindicated among his own people (the judgment for irreverence, Psalm 89:7) — the smiting (looked into the ark).

On "even he smote of the people fifty thousand and threescore and ten men: and the people lamented, because the LORD had smitten many of the people with a great slaughter," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the great slaughter and lament. Ellicott and Gill note the great slaughter and the people's lament, the mercy of the ark's return turned to judgment by their irreverence, God sanctified in them that come nigh him (the great slaughter, Leviticus 10:3; Hebrews 12:28-29) — the figure (the holiness of God approached with reverence and godly fear); the providence (the judgment, the men of Bethshemesh asking who can stand before this holy God to follow, vv.20). The verse displays God smiting Bethshemesh for irreverence to the ark.

The witnesses agree the substance: God struck the men of Bethshemesh dead for irreverently prying into the ark (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of God's holiness: he smote the men of Bethshemesh, because they had looked into the ark — the God who vindicated his honour against the heathen now vindicating it among his own people, the men of Bethshemesh in unholy curiosity looking into the ark which even the Levites might not, God smiting them with a great slaughter, his holiness not to be presumed upon nor familiarity to breed irreverence, his own people not to profane his holy things, the mercy of the return turned to judgment by their want of reverence, God greatly to be feared in the assembly of his saints (Psalm 89:7), sanctified in them that come nigh him (Leviticus 10:3), served with reverence and godly fear for he is a consuming fire (Hebrews 12:28-29). The verse displays the holiness crux. God smiting Bethshemesh for irreverence to the ark displays the crux: he smote the men of Bethshemesh, because they had looked into the ark of the LORD (Psalm 89:7; Leviticus 10:3; Hebrews 12:28-29) — the holiness of God that will not be profaned even by his own people, the reverence his presence demands, the nearer the privilege the greater the reverence God requires. The verse displays the men of Bethshemesh asking who can stand before this holy God (vv.20).
20:
"And the men of Bethshemesh said, Who is able to stand before this holy LORD God? and to whom shall he go up from us?" The very brief verse notes the men of Bethshemesh asking who could stand before the holy God. The grammatical voices note the awe. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the men of Bethshemesh said, Who is able to stand before this holy LORD God? and to whom shall he go up from us?" — the people, stricken, owned the holiness of God in awe; the and the men of Bethshemesh said, Who is able to stand before this holy LORD God? (the awe); the and to whom shall he go up from us? (the ark to be removed); the figure (the awe of the holy God that overwhelms the sinner, the sense of unworthiness; the truth that the people owned none could stand before the holy God, Malachi 3:2; the figure of the sinner's dread before the holiness of God; the awe of Bethshemesh; the holy God, vv.19). The verse holds together the awe.

On "And the men of Bethshemesh said, Who is able to stand before this holy LORD God?" the witnesses note the awe. Gill notes the people's awe before the holy God (the dread, Malachi 3:2) — the awe (who is able to stand).

On "and to whom shall he go up from us?" the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the removal: "to whom shall he go up from us?" — the ark to be sent elsewhere (the sense of unworthiness); the figure (the sinner's dread before the holiness of God); the providence (the awe, the ark sent to Kirjathjearim to follow, vv.21). The verse displays the men of Bethshemesh in awe before the holy God.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the awe — "Who is able to stand before this holy LORD God? and to whom shall he go up from us?" the awe (Keil, Gill); "And the men of Bethshemesh said, Who is able to stand before this holy LORD God?" the awe (Malachi 3:2, Gill); "and to whom shall he go up from us?" the removal (Ellicott, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the awe. The verse marks the men: in awe. The verse displays the holy God: dreaded. The verse displays the standing: impossible. The verse displays the ark: to be removed. The awe displays the sinner's dread before the holiness of God. The verse displays the unworthiness: owned. The verse displays the holiness: felt. The verse displays the dread: deep. The men of Bethshemesh in awe before the holy God displays the sinner's dread before the holiness of God: Who is able to stand before this holy LORD God? and to whom shall he go up from us? (Malachi 3:2) — the people, stricken, owning the holiness of God in awe, the sinner's dread before the holiness of God. The verse displays the messengers sent to Kirjathjearim to fetch the ark (vv.21).
21:
"And they sent messengers to the inhabitants of Kirjathjearim, saying, The Philistines have brought again the ark of the LORD; come ye down, and fetch it up to you." The very brief verse notes the men of Bethshemesh sending to Kirjathjearim to fetch the ark. The grammatical voices note the sending. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "they sent messengers to the inhabitants of Kirjathjearim... The Philistines have brought again the ark of the LORD; come ye down, and fetch it up to you" — the men of Bethshemesh sent the ark away to Kirjathjearim; the and they sent messengers to the inhabitants of Kirjathjearim, saying, The Philistines have brought again the ark of the LORD (the message); the come ye down, and fetch it up to you (fetch the ark); the figure (the ark passed on in fear, the holy presence both desired and dreaded; the truth that they sent the ark to Kirjathjearim after the smiting, 1 Samuel 7:1; the figure of the holy presence that men both seek and fear to keep; the sending; the ark to Kirjathjearim, vv.20). The verse closes the chapter of the ark's return. The verse displays the men of Bethshemesh, awed by the judgment, sending the ark on to Kirjathjearim rather than keeping the holy presence among them. The verse displays the truth that the presence of God is both the greatest blessing and, to the irreverent, a thing of dread.

On "And they sent messengers to the inhabitants of Kirjathjearim, saying, The Philistines have brought again the ark of the LORD," the witnesses note the message. Gill notes the message sent to Kirjathjearim (the sending, 1 Samuel 7:1) — the message (the ark brought again).

On "come ye down, and fetch it up to you," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the fetching: "come ye down, and fetch it up to you" — the ark to be taken to Kirjathjearim (the removal); the figure (the holy presence that men both seek and fear to keep); the providence (the sending, the ark abiding in Kirjathjearim and Samuel's reformation to follow, ch.7). The verse displays the ark sent on to Kirjathjearim.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the sending — "they sent messengers to the inhabitants of Kirjathjearim... come ye down, and fetch it up to you," the sending (Keil, Gill); "And they sent messengers to the inhabitants of Kirjathjearim, saying, The Philistines have brought again the ark of the LORD," the message (1 Samuel 7:1, Gill); "come ye down, and fetch it up to you," the fetching (Ellicott, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the sending. The verse marks Bethshemesh: sending. The verse displays Kirjathjearim: summoned. The verse displays the ark: passed on. The verse displays the awe: lingering. The sending displays the holy presence that men both seek and fear to keep. The verse displays the message: sent. The verse displays the ark: removed. The verse displays the dread: of the holy. The ark sent on to Kirjathjearim displays the holy presence that men both seek and fear to keep: they sent messengers to the inhabitants of Kirjathjearim... come ye down, and fetch it up to you (1 Samuel 7:1) — the men of Bethshemesh sending the ark to Kirjathjearim after the smiting, the holy presence that men both seek and fear to keep. The verse closes the chapter, before the ark abiding in Kirjathjearim and Samuel's reformation (ch.7).
